meta.description: Quote library for As It Fell — time-tagged verses from George B. Gardiner's Folk-Songs from Hampshire (1909)
meta.notes: Quotes marked pending-wordsmith are proposed but not yet finalised. Do not push to app until status is changed to approved.

quotes.text:
As I walked forth one summer's morn
Hard by a river side,
Where yellow cowslips did adorn
The blushing field with pride,
I spied a damsel on the grass,
More blooming than the may;
Her looks the Queen of Love surpassed,
Among the new-mown hay.
quotes.source: The New-Mown Hay (Gardiner, Folk-Songs from Hampshire)
quotes.notes: Opening stanza. Collected by George B. Gardiner; pianoforte accompaniment by Gustav von Holst.

quotes.text:
As I was a-walking one morning in spring
To hear the small birds whistle and the nightingales sing,
I heard a fair maid making sorrowful moan,
Saying, "I am a stranger and far from my home."
quotes.source: The Happy Stranger (Gardiner, Folk-Songs from Hampshire)
quotes.notes: Opening stanza. Collected by George B. Gardiner; pianoforte accompaniment by Gustav von Holst.

quotes.text:
One morning this squire all on the highway
He met the poor thresherman and unto him did say,
"You have many children and that I well do know,
How can you maintain them as well as you do?"
quotes.source: The Squire and the Thresher (Gardiner, Folk-Songs from Hampshire)
quotes.notes: Verse 2. Collected by George B. Gardiner; pianoforte accompaniment by Gustav von Holst.

quotes.text:
And when I come home from my labour at night
To my wife and my family in whom I take delight,
When my children come around me with their prittle-prattling noise,
Now these are the riches a poor man enjoys.
quotes.source: The Squire and the Thresher (Gardiner, Folk-Songs from Hampshire)
quotes.notes: Verse 5. Collected by George B. Gardiner; pianoforte accompaniment by Gustav von Holst.
